During the American Revolution, women dared to write about politics in newspapers, sometimes under their own names. At the time, that was extraordinary. Mercy Otis Warren waged war against Britain with her pen, and at the urging of John Adams, wrote the history of the Revolution under her own name. Judith Sargent Murray followed in 1790 with On the Equality of the Sexes, arguing women deserved equal education and economic independence, not just for themselves, but for the sake of civic virtue. Their ideas became the backbone of Republican Motherhood, championed by Abigail Adams: women have a national duty to raise virtuous citizens, so women themselves must be educated. Therefore, women deserve equal access to education. None of them were arguing for suffrage yet. But they were building the foundation for those who would. Episode 3 of The Women's Declaration with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG) is live now 🎬 ▶️  https://bit.ly/4svDEar  #WomensHistoryMonth  #FoundingMothers  #AmericanRevolution  #CivicsEducation  #America250

For more than 25 years, the Bill of Rights Institute has worked to equip teachers and students with the knowledge of our history, principles, and civic habits that our free society depends on. Today, we're taking the next step. Were introducing the BRI Civics Innovation Lab. The Lab is built on a simple conviction: the civics market has been underserved for too long. Not for lack of passionteachers have that in abundancebut for lack of tools that match the moment. This is the moment. The classrooms of today will shape how the next generation understands liberty, equality, and what it means to be a a good citizen. The Lab exists to make sure they get it right. Read the full story and see what the Civics Innovation Lab means for teachers, students, and the future of civic education:  https://bit.ly/4srtfwh  #CivicsEducation  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #AmericaAt250  #K12Education  #TeachCivics  #HistoryEducation

On March 4, 1865, Abraham Lincoln had every reason to gloat. The Civil War was nearly over. The Union had held. He had just won reelection, during wartime, against a general who wanted to negotiate peace with the Confederacy. He could have sounded triumphant. He didn't. Instead, Lincoln delivered one of the most morally serious speeches in American political history. He named slavery as the cause of the war, plainly, without equivocation. He acknowledged that all Americans, North and South, bore some share of moral responsibility. And then he asked a nation that had just lost 700,000 of its sons to choose reconciliation over revenge. "With malice toward none, with charity for all, let us strive on to finish the work we are in and to bind up the nation's wounds." That's not rhetoric. That's civic virtue: moderation and humility, exercised at the hardest possible moment. At the Bill of Rights Institute, we believe history education means teaching students to examine both when America lived up to its ideals and when it fell short. Lincoln's Second Inaugural does both. That's what makes it one of the most teachable primary sources in the American canon. Watch our Primary Source Essentials video on Lincoln's Second Inaugural and bring this moment to life for your students or your classroom 👇  https://bit.ly/3NaVq3D  #TeachHistory  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #PrimarySource  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #OTD  #AmericaAt250

The intellectual foundation of American women's rights wasn't written in America. In 1792, English philosopher Mary Wollstonecraft published A Vindication of the Rights of Women, and the argument she made was as simple as it was revolutionary: women are human beings. And human beings, by definition, are rights-bearers with equal moral standing. Enlightenment thinkers had already begun questioning whether women's subordination was natural or simply the result of tradition and denied opportunity. Wollstonecraft took that idea and drove it straight into the natural rights tradition: if all men possess rights simply by virtue of being human, then women, who are also human, possess those rights too. She rarely spoke of suffrage directly. She didn't need to. Her deeper claim, that women had a natural right to everything promised in the Declaration of Independence, laid the philosophical groundwork that shaped founding mothers like Abigail Adams, who called herself a follower of Wollstonecraft. Episode 2 of The Women's Declaration, our 10-part Women's History Month series with Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics on IG), is live now. 🎬  https://bit.ly/4sjt06j  #WomensHistoryMonth  #MaryWollstonecraft  #NaturalRights  #AmericanHistory  #CivicsEducation  #America250

A huge congratulations to Master Printing + Mailing for partnering with the Bill of Rights Institute to support the Power of the Printed Word initiative  donating the printing and distribution of 250,000 pocket Constitutions to 2,500 classrooms across the country. Let that sink in. In a time when civics education has never been more important, this effort helps place our founding documents directly into the hands of the next generation. That is impact. That is leadership. And that is the power of print. This initiative is a reminder that our industry does more than produce materials  we help preserve history, strengthen communities, and support education at the highest level. We also applaud the incredible Graphic Media Alliance partners who stepped up to support this effort: Konica Minolta Business Solutions U. S. A., Inc., Heidelberg North America, Case Paper, History By Mail, TECNAU, and Canon USA. When suppliers and printers align around purpose, powerful things happen. To Master Printing + Mailing  thank you for leading by example. Your generosity and commitment to meaningful work represent the very best of our industry. If you want to see print change the world, this is it.  #PowerOfThePrintedWord  #CivicsEducation  #PrintMatters  #GraphicCommunications  #IndustryLeadership

We are proud to support the Bill of Rights Institute's Power of the Printed Word Initiative by donating the printing and distribution of "250 for 250." That's 250,000 pocket constitutions supporting 2,500 government, history and civics classrooms across the country! The Constitution and Bill of Rights remain the framework and firm foundation of the American experiment. The fight for women's rights in America didn't begin in 1848. It began in 1776. Under the coverture system, American women existed largely through the legal identities of their husbands and fathers. Yet the Declaration of Independence, the same document that launched a revolution, became the tool women wielded in their centuries-long struggle for liberty and equality. 250 years later, that story is still being told. And it deserves to be told well. This March, the Bill of Rights Institute is partnering with political scientist and educator Savannah Eccles Johnston, PhD (@savypolitics) for a Women's History Month series: The Women's Declaration, tracing the arc of American women's fight from founding mothers like Abigail Adams and Mercy Otis Warren, to suffragists like Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony, to revolutionaries like Alice Paul and Ida B. Wells, to the legal minds who argued equality before the highest courts in the land. This is the story of American women. It's also the story of America itself, and the struggle to fulfill its most fundamental promise. Episode 1 is live now 👇 🎬  https://bit.ly/4u56DDp  #WomensHistoryMonth  #America250  #CivicsEducation  #AmericanHistory  #TeacherResources

The student running a small business at your school? The class that organized a community food drive? The club advocating for safer roads? They're already doing it. The MyImpact Challenge is a national civics contest, but it's probably not what you're picturing. It isn't a protest sign or a voter registration table. It's students taking meaningful action in their communities: through entrepreneurship, philanthropy, service, and problem-solving. That means the business club renting out equipment at prom. The Key Club running a book drive. The FFA chapter cleaning up a park. The AP Gov class that spent a semester pushing for safer teen driving laws in their town. All of it qualifies. Students in all 50 states can enter. Every student who submits a valid project receives recognition, not just finalists. And the national winner earns a scholarship and a platform to share their story. If you work with students who are making a difference, or who could, the next step is straightforward. 👉  https://bit.ly/3fyGGr3  #MyImpactChallenge  #CivicsEducation  #ServiceLearning  #BillOfRightsInstitute  #TeacherPD
